title-group.article-title: The impact of polystyrene nanoplastics on the chicken gut and liver: Based on transcriptomics and microbiomics

contrib-group.aff: College of Animal Science and Technology, Xinyang Agriculture and Forestry University, Xinyang, Henan Province, 464000, PR China

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc. on behalf of Poultry Science Association Inc.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

abstract.p: Nanoplastics, an emerging environmental pollutant, pose potential hazards to organisms. The impact of nanoplastics on poultry health, a crucial protein source for humans, has raised considerable concern. This study evaluated the multidimensional toxic effects of nanoplastic exposure on 240 Sanhuang chickens over a 21-day oral administration period followed by a 21-day recovery period. While nanoplastics did not affect average daily gain and average daily feed intake, they induced cardiac index elevation, indicating myocardial compensatory hypertrophy, with partial functional recovery post-exposure. Serum analysis revealed that reduced antioxidant enzyme activities and increased lipid peroxidation, and pro-inflammatory cytokines levels rose during exposure. Oxidative stress and inflammatory responses were alleviated, but IgG levels significantly declined in recovery. Transcriptome sequencing showed disrupted mitochondrial complex I function and inhibited cholesterol synthesis through abnormal expression of key genes, exacerbating cytotoxicity. Mechanistic studies confirmed toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4)/ nuclear factor kappa B (NF-κB) pathway activation driving hepatic/jejunal inflammation, alongside elevated pro-apoptotic Bax/Caspase-3, reduced anti-apoptotic B-cell lymphoma-2, and suppressed autophagy and nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2 (Nrf2) pathway. Post-exposure, the TLR4/NF-κB pathway remained activated, and autophagy and the Nrf2 pathway remained inhibited. Intestinal barrier function was impaired via downregulated tight junction proteins, increasing jejunal permeability. Jejunal villi height was abnormally elevated in the low-dose group during exposure, which decreased in the villus height / crypt depth ratio during recovery. Microbiome analysis revealed increased Proteobacteria/Escherichia-Shigella abundance during exposure and reduced beneficial bacteria. Harmful bacteria such as Flavobacteriaceae_uncultured remained highly abundant post-exposure. This study systematically demonstrates that NPs compromise avian health via two primary mechanisms: inducing inflammation, oxidative stress, apoptosis, and autophagy inhibition in the liver and jejunum; disrupting mitochondrial respiration in the liver and causing intestinal barrier dysfunction accompanied by dysbiosis. These findings offer critical evidence for evaluating the potential risks of environmental nano-pollutants in livestock and poultry industry.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Microplastics (MPs) and nanoplastics (NPs) have attracted significant interest from the scientific community in recent years as emerging environmental pollutants. Research indicates that terrestrial microplastics can migrate into freshwater ecosystems via atmospheric deposition, rainwater runoff, and surface runoff (Cai et al., 2021). These microplastics not only contribute to severe environmental pollution but also have the potential to infiltrate animal organisms, posing risks to animal health. Smaller microplastic particles, such as those measuring 5 μm, have been observed to accumulate in the gastrointestinal tract, liver, and kidneys of murine models (Deng et al., 2017). Upon entering the intestinal system, microplastics can exert detrimental effects on gut health, characterized by increased intestinal permeability, diminished mucus secretion, mucosal damage, and the disruption of intestinal villi and epithelial cells (Jin et al., 2019; Deng et al., 2020). When animals ingest mixtures containing microplastics and toxic substances attached to them, the microplastics increase the absorption and accumulation of these harmful chemicals within the animals. As a result, the concentrations of these chemicals in the organisms become much higher than those in the surrounding environment, which enhances their toxic effects (Sun et al., 2025; Wu et al., 2025).

Birds serve as effective bioindicators of environmental pollutants due to their rapid responses to contamination and their ability to reflect current exposure levels, making them particularly valuable for the monitoring of pollution. Research demonstrates that chickens are susceptible to microplastic exposure via the ingestion of contaminated feed and drinking water, as well as through direct contact with microplastic particles present in their environment (Chen et al., 2023b). Furthermore, microplastics may infiltrate the organism through dermal absorption and inhalation, potentially leading to a range of health consequences. NPs enter the body during poultry farming through these pathways. Notably, empirical studies have revealed that microplastics can threaten the intestinal barrier's integrity in chickens, resulting in intestinal inflammation and oxidative stress responses. These alterations can disrupt the equilibrium of the gut microbiota, ultimately leading to intestinal dysfunction (Yin et al., 2023; Zou et al., 2023). Recent research has demonstrated that polystyrene microplastics (PS-MPs) can influence the inflammatory response in the thymus of chickens via the nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2 (Nrf2) / nuclear factor kappa B (NF-κB) signaling pathway, which subsequently triggers autophagy and apoptosis, thereby compromising the avian immune system (Li et al., 2023). Beyond digestive and immune system effects, microplastics have been shown to adversely affect on the cardiomyocytes (Zhang et al., 2022a), liver-gut axis (Yin et al., 2023), testis (Hou et al., 2022), lung (Lu et al., 2023), kidney (Meng et al., 2022) and Muscle quality (Chen et al., 2023b) in chickens.

Nanoplastics, specifically microplastic particles measuring less than 1000 nm, surpass the detection capabilities of standard monitoring techniques, resulting in a persistent underestimation of environmental contamination. Furthermore, due to their diminutive size, these particles exhibit an enhanced ability to penetrate cellular membranes, compromising the structural integrity and functionality of organelles and leading to a variety of toxicological effects (Banerjee and Shelver, 2021). Zha et al. (2024) demonstrated that, compared to MP, foodborne and airborne NP could respectively induce greater toxicity in lung and liver. Current research suggests that the toxicological mechanisms associated with polystyrene nanoparticles (PS-NPs) within biological systems are predominantly linked to disruptions in carbohydrate and lipid metabolism, impaired growth and development, endocrine disturbances, reproductive health issues, oxidative stress, inflammatory responses, and cellular apoptosis (Heddagaard and Møller, 2020; Goodman et al., 2021; Han et al., 2023). Investigations on human small intestine epithelial cells (HIEC-6) and colon epithelial cells (CCD841CoN) exposed to polystyrene particles of varying sizes for 24 hours revealed that smaller PS-NPs were more readily internalized by cells, with the degree of cellular uptake being directly correlated with the duration of exposure. Elevated concentrations of PS-NPs have been shown to exert toxic effects characterized by increased oxidative stress levels and alterations in mitochondrial membrane potential (Zhang et al., 2022b). Research conducted by Huang et al. (2022) indicated that maternal exposure to PS-NPs during gestation and lactation could result in a reduction in the birth weight of offspring mice. Nevertheless, the specific toxicological mechanisms of NPs in avian species, particularly in the liver and intestines of chickens, remain inadequately understood.

We hypothesize that after chickens ingest polystyrene NPs, significant changes occur in gene expression in their intestines and liver. Additionally, there are alterations in the composition and function of their gut microbiota. These changes negatively impact the health of the chickens' intestines and liver. Furthermore, a significant correlation exists between the changes in gut microbiota and the alterations in liver gene expression, which together mediate the adverse effects of PS-NPs on chicken health. Consequently, this study investigated the toxicity and underlying mechanisms of varying PS-NP doses on the hepatic and intestinal systems of Guizhou chickens, aiming to provide a theoretical framework for further elucidating their mechanisms of action and developing effective prevention and control strategies.

sec.title: Experimental animals and reagents
sec.p: A group of 240 one-day-old Sanhuang chickens, with an equal number of males and females, was obtained from Gushi County Yisheng Poultry Industry Co., Ltd.

Polystyrene nanoparticles with a diameter of 500 nm (50 mg/mL) were acquired from Jiangsu Zhichuan Technology Co., Ltd. Additionally, kits for catalase (CAT), glutathione peroxidase (GSH-Px), superoxide dismutase (SOD), and malondialdehyde (MDA) were obtained from the Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute. Furthermore, tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α), interleukin-1β (IL-1β), interleukin-10 (IL-10), and immunoglobulin G (IgG) were sourced from Jiangsu Meiyuan Industrial Co., Ltd.

sec.title: Animal grouping and treatment
sec.p:
A total of 240 chickens were subjected to a pre-feeding period of one week, after which they were weighed and subsequently assigned to four experimental groups: the NC group, MPD group, MPZ group, and MPG group. Each group consisted of six replicates, with ten chickens per replicate. The nutritional composition of the basal diet is detailed in Table 1S. The chickens were provided with unrestricted access to water and managed according to standard feeding protocols in cage-rearing systems. The housing environment was maintained through regular cleaning, and the chickens were exposed to natural light and ventilation daily. For the oral administration of nanoplastics, the initial dosing volumes for the MPD, MPZ, and MPG groups were set at 0.1 mL (5 mg/mL), 0.2 mL (10 mg/mL), and 0.4 mL (20 mg/mL), respectively. The drug was administered every 48 hours. Each dosage was repeated for two consecutive treatments before being doubled in the following cycle (Figure 1A). The dosage employed in this study was established in accordance with the findings reported by Lai et al. (Lai et al., 2021) and Zha et al. (Zha et al., 2024). The NC group was given the same dose of 0.9% normal saline as the treatment group. This dosing regimen was maintained for a duration of 21 days, after which no further NP was administered for 21 days. The overall duration of the experiment was 42 days, during which the average daily feed intake (ADFI), feed-to-gain (F/G), and average daily weight gain (ADG) was calculated at both the 21-day and 42-day marks using the formula ADG (g/d) = weight gain / (number of experimental days × number of chicks). A total of six rats were randomly selected from each replicate within each group. Blood samples were collected via cardiac puncture, and the chickens were euthanized through severance of the jugular vein under anesthesia. Tissues collected included the liver, heart, spleen, bursa of Fabricius, jejunal tissue, and cecal contents, with a fasting period of one day prior to euthanasia. The designations for the groups sampled at 21 days were NC21, MP21D, MP21Z, and MP21G, while those for the 42-day sampling were NC42, MP42D, MP42Z, and MP42G, with each group comprising six chickens.Table 1

sec.title: Determination of serum indicators
sec.p: Blood samples obtained from the heart were refrigerated at 4°C overnight, after which they were subjected to centrifugation at 4°C at a speed of 3,500 revolutions per minute for 15 minutes to facilitate serum separation. Subsequently, the antioxidant parameters, including GSH-Px, CAT, SOD, and MDA, along with the immune markers IL-1β, TNF-α, IL-10, and IgG, were analyzed in accordance with the specified protocols for each respective assay.

sec.title: Organ index
sec.p: Following the euthanasia of the chicken and blood collection, the liver, heart, spleen, and bursa of Fabricius were excised, while the adjacent fascia and tissues were discarded. Residual blood was absorbed using filter paper, after which the organs were weighed to determine the immune organ index. The liver was subsequently preserved in liquid nitrogen. The organ index was computed using the formula: organ index (g/kg) =organ weight (g) / body weight (kg).

sec.title: Measurement of morphological and structural parameters of jejunal tissue
sec.p: Following the collection of jejunal tissue, a segment was preserved in liquid nitrogen, while another segment was fixed in a 4% paraformaldehyde solution. The jejunal tissue samples underwent a series of processing steps, including dehydration, paraffin infiltration, embedding, sectioning at a thickness of 5 μm, and staining with hematoxylin and eosin (H.E.). Subsequent to these procedures, the samples were examined microscopically. According to the measurement method of Segú et al. (2022) villus height (VH) and crypt depth (CD) were measured using image acquisition software (ZEISS ZEN 3.1, Germany), from which the VH/CD ratio was calculated.

sec.title: Fluorescence quantitative RT-PCR
sec.p: Total RNA was extracted from chicken liver and jejunum tissues according to the instructions of the RNA extraction solution (Wuhan Servicebio Technology Co., Ltd.). Total RNA was reverse transcribed into cDNA following the instructions of the reverse transcription kit (Wuhan Servicebio Technology Co., Ltd.). The relative expression levels of mRNA for genes in the liver and jejunum were detected using fluorescence quantitative RT-PCR (RT-qPCR), with β-actin as the internal reference gene. Detailed information about the genes can be found in Table 2S, and the primers were synthesized by Suzhou Jinweizhi Biotechnology Co., Ltd. The RT-qPCR reaction system (20 μL) consisted of 10 μL of 2 × SYBR Green qPCR Master Mix, 2.0 μL of cDNA, 0.4 μL of upstream and downstream primers each, and 7.2 μL of ddH2O. The reaction program was as follows: 95°C for 30 s; 95°C for 15 s, 60°C for 30 s, repeated for 40 cycles.

sec.title: 16S rDNA sequencing and analysis of cecal microbiota
sec.p: The cecal contents were cryopreserved in liquid nitrogen and subsequently dispatched to Shanghai Oringene Biomedical Technology Co., Ltd. for 16S rDNA V3-V4 sequencing employing the Illumina HiSeq 2500 sequencing platform. The initial 16S rDNA sequences underwent filtration and assembly using the QIIME V1.9.0 and FLASH software packages. High-quality sequences were aligned with the Silva reference database (https://www.arb-silva.de/) and clustered into operational taxonomic units (OTUs) at a 97% similarity threshold utilizing the UCLUST algorithm (Schloss and Westcott, 2011). Species annotation was executed based on the Ribosomal Database Project (RDP) database, and alpha diversity metrics (ACE, Chao, Shannon, Simpson index) were assessed using the QIIME 2 platform (version 2021.08). ACE and Chao indices estimate microbial richness, with higher values indicating greater abundance. Simpson and Shannon indices assess microbial diversity within samples—higher Simpson values indicate lower community diversity, while higher Shannon values denote greater diversity. Hierarchical clustering analysis of the microbiota structure was performed via the Bray-Curtis algorithm, facilitating a comparison of the microbiota structure and distribution between the two groups at both the phylum and genus levels. Functional predictions of the cecal microbiota were conducted using PICRUSt v1.1.327 software, which provided relevant Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) functional information. Differences in microbial abundance and functionality between the groups were analyzed using the t-test in STAMP 2.1.3.

sec.title: Liver transcriptome sequencing
sec.p: Liver samples were sent to Shanghai Yuanxin Biotechnology Co., Ltd. for transcriptome sequencing, with three biological replicates set for each group. After RNA extraction, quality control, library construction, and passing library quality inspection, all libraries were sequenced using the Illumina Novaseq™ 6000 platform. The sequencing data were processed to remove adapters and low-quality reads, and Cutadapt was used to filter out unqualified sequences to obtain valid data. A comprehensive evaluation was conducted prior to further analysis.

The quality-controlled data were aligned to the chicken GRCg7b reference genome (source: https://asia.ensembl.org/Gallus_gallus/Info/Index) using the HISAT2 algorithm, and the FPKM values of genes were obtained using StringTie (v2.1.4). Differential expression results between groups were obtained using DESeq2 (http://bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/DESeq2.html). Differentially expressed genes (DEGs) were identified using the screening criteria of FDR < 0.05 and |log2FoldChange| > 1. KEGG functional annotation and enrichment analysis were performed on the DEGs.

We used the FPKM values of all genes in the liver from both the control and experimental groups as the gene matrix, with the dose added as the phenotype matrix. Utilizing the gene expression profiles, we calculated the Median Absolute Deviation (MAD) for each gene and removed the bottom 50% of genes with the lowest MAD. The R package Weighted Correlation Network Analysis (WGCNA) was employed to eliminate outlier genes and samples using the goodSamplesGenes method, followed by constructing a scale-free co-expression network (http://sangerbox.com/home.html). To further identify relevant core genes, we performed Protein-Protein Interaction (PPI) network analysis using the STRING database (https://STRING-db.org/) with a confidence threshold of 0.4, and visualized the core gene network expression using Cytoscape software. Additionally, Friends analysis of the core genes was conducted using the R package GOSemSim (https://www.xiantaozi.com/). This method utilizes network topology to calculate the importance of each gene, thus identifying key genes from the list. Genes rank in descending order according to their mean similarity to all other genes.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Statistical analysis of ADG, serum indices, organ indices, and gene mRNA expression levels was performed using GraphPad Prism 9 software (GraphPad Software, San Diego, California, USA). Group data were analyzed by one-way ANOVA followed by Tukey-Kramer multiple comparison tests, with results expressed as “mean ± standard error”. A P-value <0.05 was considered statistically significant between groups.

sec.title: Growth performance and serum parameters
sec.p: Our findings indicate that NPs yielded no significant effect on chicken average daily feed intake (ADFI) (P > 0.05, Table 1). During days 1–21 of NP treatment, the ADG of the MPG group was significantly reduced, and the F/G ratio in the MPZ and MPG groups was significantly higher than that in the NC group (P < 0.05, Table 1). Both NPs reduced the liver and bursa of Fabricius indices in the treatment groups (P > 0.05, Fig. 1B and 1D). Post-exposure, The indices for chicken liver and bursa of Fabricius remained lower than those of the NC42 group. Interestingly, NPs significantly increased the cardiac index in the MP21D and MP21G groups (P < 0.05, Fig. 1C). Besides, NPs induced oxidative stress in chicken blood, significantly inhibiting the activity of serum antioxidant enzymes GSH-Px, CAT, and SOD in the MP21G groups, while significantly increasing serum MDA levels in the treatment groups (P < 0.05, Fig. 1F–I). Post-exposure, oxidative stress was partially alleviated, with only the MP42Z group showing a significant inhibitory effect on serum CAT activity (P < 0.05, Fig. 1G). Our results also demonstrated that NPs could impair serum immune function, with significant increases in pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-1β and TNF-α in the MP21G groups (P < 0.05, Fig. 1J–K). Serum IL-10 levels were elevated at both 21 days and 42 days, with no significant difference observed between the two time points (P < 0.05, Fig. 1L). Even after discontinuation, NPs s continued to exhibit a notable inhibitory effect on serum IgG levels in the MP21G groups (P < 0.05, Fig. 1M).

sec.title: Transcriptome analysis on the liver
sec.p: Using Illumina paired-end sequencing technology, we conducted transcriptome sequencing on chicken liver tissues after 21 days of NP exposure. The differential gene expression analysis results are shown in Fig. S1. Compared to the NC21 group, the MP21D group exhibited 18 downregulated DEGs and 23 upregulated DEGs; the MP21Z group had 35 downregulated DEGs and 32 upregulated DEGs; and the MP21G group showed 43 downregulated DEGs and 28 upregulated DEGs. During the MP21D vs. NC21 comparison, 33 pathways were enriched in KEGG, primarily including ECM-receptor interaction and Cell adhesion molecules (CAMs) (Fig. S1B). During the MP21Z vs. NC21 comparison, 74 pathways were enriched in KEGG, mainly involving Glycerophospholipid metabolism and Metabolic pathways (Fig. S1D). During the MP21G vs. NC21 comparison, 86 pathways were enriched in KEGG, also dominated by Glycerophospholipid metabolism and Metabolic pathways (Fig. S1F).

The differential gene expression patterns in hepatic tissue, analyzed after a 21-day recovery period, are shown in Fig. S2. Compared to the NC42 group, the MP42D group comprised 69 downregulated and 89 upregulated DEGs; the MP42Z group comprised 104 downregulated and 352 upregulated DEGs; and the MP42G group comprised 119 downregulated and 535 upregulated DEGs. During the comparison MP21D vs. NC21, 151 pathways were enriched in KEGG, with 2 significantly enriched pathways (Padj < 0.05), mainly including Metabolic pathways and Steroid biosynthesis (Fig. S2B). A comparison of MP21Z vs. NC21 demonstrated 249 pathways were enriched in KEGG, with 7 significantly enriched pathways (Padj < 0.05), mainly including Osteoclast differentiation, PI3K-Akt signaling pathway, Steroid biosynthesis, VEGF signaling pathway, Focal adhesion, Fc epsilon RI signaling pathway, and Leukocyte transendothelial migration (Fig. S2D). A comparison of MP21G vs. NC21 revealed that 265 pathways were enriched in KEGG, with 21 significantly enriched pathways (Padj < 0.05), mainly including Leukocyte transendothelial migration, Fc epsilon RI signaling pathway, HIF-1 signaling pathway, Fc gamma R-mediated phagocytosis, Inflammatory mediator regulation of TRP channels, Cell adhesion molecules (CAMs), and Toll-like receptor signaling pathway (Fig. S2F).

Next, RNA-Seq data derived from three dosage cohorts subjected to nanoplastic exposure for 21 days, followed by a 21-day recovery phase, were utilized. From the expression matrix, the top 20,000 genes were selected based on the Median Absolute Deviation (MAD) value for WGCNA construction. To ensure that the interaction strength between genes followed a scale-free distribution, a soft threshold βpower=5 (Fig. S3A and Fig. S3B) was selected to construct the co-expression matrix. Module merging was then performed, resulting in 34 modules (Fig. S3C). To identify key genes related to nanoplastic-induced liver injury, we screened the modules based on different dose levels as traits, using the criteria |cor| > 0.4 and P < 0.05. Among the 34 co-expression modules, at 21 days, the three dose groups comprised 4, 1, and 4 small modules meeting the screening criteria, respectively. At 42 days, the three dose groups comprised 5, 1, and 4 small modules meeting the criteria (Fig. S3D). Consequently, genes from a total of 19 small modules were selected for further analysis.

To further screen relevant core genes, PPI network analysis was performed using the STRING database, and the top 20 key genes in each experimental group were visualized using the CytoHubba plugin in Cytoscape (Fig. 2A–F). We also applied the Friends analysis method to further analyze the extracted 50 genes and identify the key genes. In the MP21D group, the top three key genes were NDUFB6 (mean=0.76), NDUFB5 (mean=0.74), NDUFS8 (mean=0.73) (Fig. 2G), whereas the MP21Z group had CCNB1 (mean=0.59), MAD2L1 (mean=0.58), and CDCA3 (mean=0.58) (Fig. 2H), Similarly, the MP21G group showed MSMO1 (mean=0.56), DHCR7 (mean=0.51), and SQLE (mean=0.51) (Fig. 2I). Between the MP42D and MP42G groups, NDUFB6 (mean=0.77, 0.79, respectively), NDUFB5 (mean=0.75, 0.76, respectively), and NDUFS8 (mean=0.75, 0.75, respectively) were again the dominant genes (Fig. 2J, Fig. 2L), while the MP42Z group displayed high expression of MSMO1 (mean=0.62), DHCR7 (mean=0.58), and CYP51A1 (mean=0.56) (Fig. 2K). The FPKM values of selected key genes were subjected to one-way ANOVA, revealing that at 42 days, NDUFS8, MSMO1, and CYP51A1 were significantly lower than the control group (P < 0.05) (Fig. 2O–P, Fig. 2R, Fig. 2S).Fig. 2
Identification of key genes. (A, B, and C) The PPI network of core targets for MP21D vs. NC21, MP21Z vs. NC21, and MP21G vs. NC21, respectively. (D, E, and F) The PPI network of KEGG core targets for MP42D vs. NC42, MP42Z vs. NC42, and MP42G vs. NC42, respectively. (G, H, and I) The Friends analysis plots for MP21D vs. NC21, MP21Z vs. NC21, and MP21G vs. NC21, respectively. (J, K, and L) The Friends analysis plots for KEGG of MP42D vs. NC42, MP42Z vs. NC42, and MP42G vs. NC42, respectively. Genes are ranked in descending order based on the mean similarity with other genes, with the top gene indicating the highest similarity and being identified as the key gene. (M-T) The expression analysis of key genes in the transcriptome. The results are expressed as “mean ± standard error”. *, P < 0.05, **, P < 0.01 vs NC group. Unmarked means P > 0.05 with NC group. n = 3.Fig 2 dummy alt text

sec.title: Effects of nanoplastics on the TLR4/NF-κB and Nrf2 signaling pathways, apoptosis, and autophagy in chicken liver and jejunum
sec.p:
In this study, chicken exposure to nanoplastics significantly upregulated liver and jejunum expression levels of TLR4, MYD88, TRIF, IKK-β (jejunum), NF-κB, IL-1β, TNF-α, PIK3CA, AKT, Bax (liver), and Caspase-3 (P < 0.05) (Fig. 3A–I, Fig. 4A–I, Fig. 3K–L, Fig. 4K–L). Nanoplastics were found to significantly reduce Bcl-2 expression levels in the liver of chickens (P < 0.05) (Fig. 3J). They also significantly decreased Bcl-2 expression in the jejunum of the MP21G group (P < 0.05) (Fig. 4J). Our results confirmed that in the expression levels of Beclin1, ATG5, LC3-I, LC3-II, Nrf2, NQO1, CAT, SOD, GPX-1, and HO-1 in chicken liver and jejunum exposed to nanoplastics (P < 0.05) (Fig. 3M–V, Fig. 4M–V).Fig. 3
Measurement results of the effect of nanoplastic feeding for 21 days on liver gene mRNA expression levels. The results are expressed as “mean ± standard error”. *, P < 0.05, **, P < 0.01 vs NC group. Unmarked means P > 0.05 with NC group. n = 6.Fig 3 dummy alt textFig. 4
Measurement results of the effect of nanoplastic feeding for 21 days on jejunal gene mRNA expression levels. The results are expressed as “mean ± standard error”. *, P < 0.05, **, P < 0.01 vs NC group. Unmarked means P > 0.05 with NC group. n = 6.Fig 4 dummy alt text

To verify whether chickens could effectively recover from the negative effects of nanoplastics post-exposure, analyses conducted after 21 days of recovery showed that the TLR4/NF-κB signaling pathway remained activated. The expression levels of TLR4, MYD88, TRIF, IKK-β (jejunum), NF-κB, IL-1β, and TNF-α genes in the liver and jejunum were significantly higher than those in the NC42 group (Fig. 5A–G, Fig. 6A–G). The apoptotic process in the liver was also in an active state, with significantly reduced Bcl-2 gene expression and significantly increased Bax and Caspase-3 mRNA levels compared to the NC42 group (P < 0.05) (Fig. 5J–L). In contrast, apoptosis in the jejunum showed significant improvement, with Bcl-2 gene expression significantly increased and Caspase-3 mRNA levels significantly lower than the NC42 group (P < 0.05) (Fig. 6J–L). Regarding autophagy, the low-dose (MP42D) group exhibited enhanced autophagy in the liver and jejunum, with Beclin1, LC3-I, and LC3-II gene expression significantly higher than the NC42 group. However, autophagy remained suppressed in the intermediate- and high-dose (MP42Z and MP42G) groups, with Beclin1, ATG5, LC3-I, and LC3-II gene expression levels significantly lower than the NC42 group (P < 0.05) (Fig. 5M–P, Fig. 6M–P). The Nrf2 signaling pathway in the liver and jejunum demonstrated persistent inhibition, with Nrf2, NQO1, CAT (liver), SOD, and GPX-1 gene expression levels significantly lower than the NC42 group (P < 0.05) (Fig. 5Q–U, Fig. 6Q–U).Fig. 5
The measurement results of the effect of stopping the administration of nanoplastics for 21 days (in a 42-day experiment) on the mRNA expression levels in the liver. The results are expressed as “mean ± standard error”. *, P < 0.05, **, P < 0.01 vs NC group. Unmarked means P > 0.05 with NC group. n = 6.Fig 5 dummy alt textFig. 6
The measurement results of the effect of stopping the administration of nanoplastics for 21 days (in a 42-day experiment) on the mRNA expression levels in the jejunum. The results are expressed as “mean ± standard error”. *, P < 0.05, **, P < 0.01 vs NC group. Unmarked means P > 0.05 with NC group. n = 6.Fig 6 dummy alt text

sec.title: The impact of nanoplastics on the intestinal mucosal epithelial barrier in chickens
sec.p:
Our research indicated that nanoplastics could significantly downregulate the gene expression levels of Mucin2, Claudin1, Occludin (MP21G Group), and ZO-1 (P < 0.05) (Fig. 7A–D). After a 21-day recovery period, compared to the control group (NC42 group), the impact of nanoplastics on tight junction-related genes showed no significant difference (Fig. 7E–H). Interestingly, our findings revealed that after 21 days of nanoplastic exposure, the jejunal villus height significantly increased in both the MP21D and MP21Z groups, while the crypt depth significantly increased in the MP21D group (P < 0.05) (Fig. 7I–K, Fig. S4). After a 21-day withdrawal period from nanoplastic exposure, compared to the NC42 group, the MP42G group showed significantly reduced jejunal VH and VH/CD ratio, while the MP42Z and MP42G groups exhibited significantly increased jejunal CD (P < 0.05) (Fig. 7L–N, Fig. S4).Fig. 7
Measurement results of the effects of nanoplastics on jejunal mechanical barrier genes. (A-H) Tight junction protein genes. (I-N) Intestinal morphology. ZO-1, zonula occludens-1; VH, villus length; CD, crypt depth; VH/CD, ratio of villus length to crypt depth. The results are expressed as “mean ± standard error”. *, P < 0.05, **, P < 0.01 vs NC group. Unmarked means P > 0.05 with NC group. n = 6.Fig 7 dummy alt text

sec.title: The impact of nanoplastics on the gut microbiota of chickens
sec.p:
Although no significant differences were observed in Alpha diversity metrics among groups (P > 0.05), nanoplastics increased microbial richness and diversity, as illustrated in Venn plots (Fig. 8A–B).Fig. 8
Effects of 21-day nanoplastic feeding on cecal microbiota. (A) Microbial alpha diversity in the cecum. (B) Venn diagram. (C) Relative abundance of microbiota at the phylum level. (E) Relative abundance of microbiota at the genus level. (D) Differential analysis of microbiota abundance at the phylum level. (F) Differential analysis of microbiota abundance at the genus level. (G) STAMP differential analysis based on PICRUSt functional prediction using the KEGG database. n = 4.Fig 8 dummy alt text

Microbial community composition analysis revealed that both the control and experimental groups were predominantly composed of Firmicutes, Bacteroidota, Proteobacteria, and Actinobacteriota at the phylum level (Fig. 8C). STAMP software analysis showed that compared to the NC21 group, the abundance of the dominant bacterial phylum Proteobacteria significantly increased in the MP21D and MP21Z groups, while the abundance of Actinobacteriota significantly rose in the MP21Z group (p < 0.05, Fig. 8D).

At the genus level, the top 10 dominant bacteria in the chicken intestinal microbiota across all groups were Faecalibacterium, Alistipes, Lachnospiraceae_unclassified, Clostridia_UCG-014_norank, Clostridia_vadinBB60_group_norank, Escherichia-Shigella, Bacteroides, Oscillospiraceae_uncultured, [Ruminococcus]_torques_group, and Ruminococcaceae_uncultured (Fig. 8E). Nanoplastics significantly increased the abundance of Anaeroplasma, Christensenellaceae R-7 group, Escherichia-Shigella, and Collinsella in the cecum (P < 0.05, Fig. 8F).

Functional analysis of the microbiota was performed using PICRUSt2 software, and sequencing data were aligned against the KEGG database. The predictive analysis revealed 38 KEGG Level 2 (L2) pathways (Fig. S5), including those related to aging, metabolism, antimicrobial activity, cancer, immune diseases, and neural regeneration. STAMP differential analysis (Fig. 8G) showed that compared to the NC21 group, the abundance of Nervous System functional genes in the gut microbiota of the MP21D group was significantly reduced, while the abundance of Genetic Information Processing and Infectious Diseases functional genes significantly increased (P < 0.05). In the MP21Z group, the abundance of Nervous System and Poorly Characterized functional genes in the gut microbiota significantly decreased, whereas the abundance of Infectious Diseases functional genes significantly increased (P < 0.05).

After 21 days of discontinuation of nanoplastics, there were no significant differences in alpha diversity indices among the groups (P > 0.05) (Fig. 9A–B). Research into the makeup of microbial communities demonstrated that both the control and experimental groups were predominantly dominated by Bacteroidota, Firmicutes, Proteobacteria, Campylobacterota, and Actinobacteriota at the phylum level (Fig. 9C). STAMP analysis showed no significant differences in the abundance of dominant bacterial taxa among the groups (Fig. 9D). At the genus level, the top 10 dominant bacterial taxa in the intestinal microbiota of all groups were Bacteroides, Barnesiella, Alistipes, Faecalibacterium, Clostridia_UCG-014_norank, Lachnospiraceae_unclassified, [Ruminococcus]_torques_group, Clostridia_vadinBB60_group_norank, Coprobacter, and Ruminococcaceae_uncultured. Compared to the NC42 group, the experimental group showed a significant increase in the abundance of Flavobacteriaceae_uncultured, [Eubacterium]_coprostanoligenes_group_norank, and Phascolarctobacterium, while Barnesiella and Shuttleworthia were significantly reduced (P < 0.05) (Fig. 9E–F).Fig. 9
Effects of nanoplastics after 21 days of cessation (day 42 of the experiment) on cecal microbiota. (A) Microbial alpha diversity in the cecum. (B) Venn diagram. (C) Relative abundance of microbiota at the phylum level. (E) Relative abundance of microbiota at the genus level. (D) Differential analysis of microbiota abundance at the phylum level. (F) Differential analysis of microbiota abundance at the genus level. (G) STAMP differential analysis based on PICRUSt functional prediction using the KEGG database. n = 4.Fig 9 dummy alt text

The predictive analysis results revealed 39 KEGG secondary functional (L2) pathways (Fig. S6). Compared to the NC42 group, the nanoplastic group showed a significantly reduced abundance of functional genes related to Cellular Processes and Signaling, Enzyme Families, Excretory System, Nervous System, Endocrine System, and Transport and Catabolism, while the abundance of functional genes associated with Translation, Environmental Adaptation, and Genetic Information Processing significantly increased (P < 0.05) (Fig. 9G).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Plastics encompass a wide range of synthetic or natural high polymers. Different types of plastics are essential for industrial manufacturing and daily living. Plastics exhibit significant resistance to biodegradation, typically breaking down into micro- or nano-sized particles through physical processes that may take decades or even centuries (Pivokonsky et al., 2018). This persistence has prompted an increasing number of researchers to investigate the effects of MPs on various organisms (Yuan et al., 2022). Besides, MPs are known to transport hazardous substances, including toxic additives and heavy metals (Tiwari et al., 2019). Particles smaller than 150 μm can be absorbed by organisms, penetrating cellular structures (Yuan et al., 2022), and can accumulate within biological food webs. Numerous studies have demonstrated that avian species, poultry, and fish ingest nanoparticles, which can subsequently be absorbed by the digestive system and transported from the circulatory system to various organs (Xing et al., 2020). Notably, the accumulation of particles ranging from nano to micron size has been observed in the lungs. Collectively, these findings indicate that the ingestion of microplastics poses significant health risks to animal organisms.

Our findings indicate that nanoplastics significantly increased the cardiac index. The cardiac enlargement may be attributed to microplastics disrupting the normal physiological morphology of myocardial tissue and triggering myocardial dysplasia in birds (Zhang et al., 2022a), leading to compensatory response. After discontinuation of nanoplastic exposure, cardiac function exhibited recovery to some extent, with no significant differences in cardiac index observed among the groups. Besides, nanoplastics induced oxidative stress in chicken blood. Post-exposure, oxidative stress was partially alleviated, with only the intermediate-dose group showing a significant inhibitory effect on serum CAT activity. Concurrently, nanoplastics could impair serum immune function, with significant increases in pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-1β and TNF-α. Even after discontinuation, nanoplastics continued to exhibit a notable inhibitory effect on serum IgG levels. In summary, High dose nanoplastics can induce oxidative stress and suppress immune function in serum. Although oxidative stress was substantially alleviated after discontinuation, the inhibitory effect on blood immune function persisted to some extent.

The liver, as the primary organ for biological metabolism, detoxification of foreign substances, and excretion of harmful compounds, is a target organ for numerous toxic and hazardous substances, including microplastics. Microplastics enter the body through respiration, skin contact, and ingestion of food or water, subsequently reaching the liver via the bloodstream. However, research on the effects of NPs on poultry liver remains limited. Therefore, we employed RNA-Seq transcriptome sequencing to examine gene expression variations in chicken liver exposed to NPs. revealing the comprehensive impact and mechanisms of NPs on chickens. The study results indicated that both the medium-dose and high-dose groups exhibited significant enrichment in glycerophospholipid metabolism pathways, as identified through KEGG analysis. Glycerophospholipid metabolism influences inflammation by producing both pro-inflammatory and anti-inflammatory mediators, essential for regulating responses (O'Donnell et al., 2018). Phosphatidylinositol can be phosphorylated to PIP2, which is cleaved into DAG and IP3 upon hormonal stimulation. PI3K generates PIP3, activating the AKT pathway to support tumor cell survival (He et al., 2025). Polyunsaturated fatty acids in glycerophospholipids are susceptible to oxidation, causing cellular damage, while phospholipids can help reduce oxidative stress by modulating antioxidant enzyme activity (Hu et al., 2025). Abnormal glycerophospholipid metabolism produces specific lipid metabolites that may directly or indirectly affect the activity of the NF-κB signaling pathway. For example, in a study investigating the treatment of ulcerative colitis with Patrinia villosa, the therapeutic effect of Patrinia villosa was associated with the regulation of multiple metabolic pathways, including glycerophospholipid metabolism, and this regulation, in turn, inhibited the overactivation of the NF-κB signaling pathway (Wang et al., 2022). ECM-receptor interaction is one of the core signaling pathways by which cells sense and respond to their microenvironment. It plays a key role in maintaining tissue homeostasis, regulating cell behavior, and contributing to the occurrence and progression of various diseases (Yan et al., 2022; Chen et al., 2023a). Abnormal activation of this pathway is a central mechanism driving tissue fibrosis (Chen et al., 2023a; Zhao et al., 2025). The ECM-receptor interaction pathway engages in extensive crosstalk with other key signaling pathways, together forming a complex regulatory network. It often works synergistically with signaling pathways such as PI3K-Akt, and the focal adhesion pathway to amplify its biological effects (Zhu et al., 2026).

After 21 days of recovery, both the low-, medium-, and high-dose groups were significantly enriched in the KEGG pathway analysis for Steroid biosynthesis and Fc epsilon RI signaling pathway. Cholesterol is a precursor for steroid hormone synthesis, and microplastic exposure may alter cholesterol levels, thereby affecting steroid synthesis (Kehinde et al., 2024). Microplastics and the chemicals they adsorb can directly or indirectly influence the activity of enzymes involved in steroid synthesis, such as cytochrome P450 enzymes (Ye et al., 2025). Oxidative stress and inflammatory responses induced by microplastic exposure may damage steroid-synthesizing cells, further disrupting steroid production (van Boxel et al., 2024). Exposure to PS-MPs activates the NF-κB signaling pathway in bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs), leading to a significant upregulation of inflammatory factors, accelerated BMSC senescence, and inhibition of their osteogenic differentiation and extracellular mineralization. Senescent BMSCs excessively secrete RANKL, promoting osteoclast formation (Pan et al., 2025). Mast cells are the primary expressers of Fc epsilon RI, and micro/nanoplastics may indirectly affect the Fc epsilon RI signaling pathway by disrupting mast cell function. Studies have shown that microplastics can activate multiple signaling pathways, such as the PI3K-Akt-mTOR pathway, which shares key molecules with the Fc epsilon RI signaling pathway (Xu et al., 2025). M/NPs can induce endothelial cells to release pro-inflammatory factors (e.g., IL-6, TNF-α), enhancing leukocyte chemotaxis via the NF-κB pathway. However, excessive inflammatory signaling may compromise endothelial barrier integrity, leading to disordered leukocyte infiltration and exacerbating tissue damage. For example, nanoplastic-treated macrophages release large amounts of IL-1β, which activates endothelial cells through paracrine signaling, promoting neutrophil transendothelial migration (Yang et al., 2023).

To further screen for core relevant genes, we applied Weighted Gene Co-expression Network Analysis (WGCNA), Protein-Protein Interaction (PPI) network analysis, and Friends analysis to the various dose groups after 21 days of nano-plastic exposure and 21 days post-withdrawal. The key genes identified were NDUFB6, NDUFB5, NDUFS8, CCNB1, MAD2L1, CDCA3, MSMO1, DHCR7, SQLE, and CYP51A1. NDUFB6, NDUFB5, and NDUFS8 are essential components of mitochondrial respiratory chain complex I, collectively involved in the electron transfer process from NADH to ubiquinone, playing an indispensable role in oxidative phosphorylation. As accessory and core subunits of complex I, they are crucial for structural stability and electron transport (Milenkovic et al., 2017; Vercellino and Sazanov, 2022). It is now understood that MSMO1, DHCR7, CYP51A1, and SQLE are key enzymes in the cholesterol synthesis pathway, participating in different steps and are vital for maintaining intracellular cholesterol levels. Besides, enzymes such as MSMO1, DHCR7, CYP51A1, EBP, and SC5D collectively form a regulatory network for ferroptosis by modulating 7-dehydrocholesterol (7-DHC) levels, influencing cellular sensitivity to ferroptosis (Li et al., 2024a). Among these, MSMO1, CYP51A1, EBP, and SC5D are potential inhibitors of ferroptosis, while DHCR7 is a promoter of ferroptosis. Building upon these findings, we speculate that nanoplastics may physically disrupt the inner mitochondrial membrane structure or induce oxidative stress, leading to decreased stability of the iron-sulfur clusters in NDUFS8, impaired electron transport, and subsequent excessive generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), thereby activating the ferroptosis pathway. The significant reduction in MSMO1 and CYP51A1 expression levels also suggests that nanoplastics enhance ferroptosis sensitivity by interfering with the cholesterol synthesis pathway and lowering 7-DHC levels, thereby exacerbating their cytotoxicity. The specific regulatory mechanisms involved require further in-depth study.

The TLR family plays a crucial role in innate immune recognition by identifying specific patterns of microbial components, known as pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs). When pathogens on the cell membrane are recognized by TLRs, signaling cascades such as myeloid differentiation factor 88 (MyD88), NF-κB, and mitogen-activated protein kinases (MAPK) are activated, ultimately inducing the production of TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6 (Mukherjee et al., 2016). Studies have shown that the TLR pathway is a primary target of environmental pollutants, as it triggers inflammatory responses associated with PAMPs and mediates immune system dysfunction in fish (Yang et al., 2021). Our findings demonstrate that chickens exposed to nanoplastics activate the TLR4/NF-κB signaling pathway, which significantly increases the expression levels of associated genes in the liver and ileum. This activation induces pronounced inflammatory responses in these organs, linking nanoplastic exposure to systemic inflammation in poultry. Similarly, exposure to microplastics also triggered inflammatory reactions in the thymus and kidneys of chickens via activation of the NF-κB signaling pathway (Meng et al., 2022; Li et al., 2023).

The PI3K/AKT pathway, as a crucial intracellular signaling pathway, is involved in regulating protein synthesis, energy metabolism, and cell proliferation, among other processes. PI3K plays a significant role in the signal transduction of growth factors, while AKT is a key downstream target in the PI3K/AKT signaling pathway, serving as a pivotal regulator in cell proliferation, growth, and apoptosis. Its activated form modulates apoptosis by altering the activity of transcription factors or inhibiting the phosphorylation of apoptotic signaling proteins (Deng and Zhou, 2023; Zhan et al., 2024). In our study, in chickens exposed to nanoplastics, the expression levels of PIK3CA and AKT genes in the liver and intestines significantly increased in the high-dose group. Apoptosis is a programmed cell death process. The Bcl-2 family (including the anti-apoptotic factor Bcl-2 and the pro-apoptotic factor Bax) and the executioner caspase, Caspase-3, are key regulators of apoptosis (Glover et al., 2024). In this study, nanoplastics were found to significantly reduce Bcl-2 expression while markedly increasing Bax (the liver alone) and Caspase-3 mRNA levels in the liver and jejunum of chickens. Our findings aligned with those of Lu et al. (2023), where increased expression of BAX/BCL-2 and Caspase-3 in PS-MP-exposed lung tissues led to apoptosis.

Autophagy is regulated by the coordinated action of multiple genes, proteins, and signaling pathways, among which the PI3K/Akt signaling pathway is a classic negative regulator of autophagy (Heras-Sandoval et al., 2014). The activation of the PI3K/Akt signaling pathway can inhibit cellular autophagy via modulation of downstream autophagy-related proteins, whereas inhibition of this pathway promotes autophagy (Ma et al., 2021). Our results confirmed that the up-regulation of PI3K/Akt signaling pathway gene expression levels led to a significant reduction in the expression levels of autophagy-related genes Beclin1, ATG5, LC3-I, and LC3-II in chicken liver and jejunum exposed to nanoplastics, indicating that long-term exposure to nanoplastics significantly suppressed autophagy. Zhang et al. (2022a) consistently found that exposure to microplastics for 42 days inhibited myocardial autophagy in chickens.

Antioxidant stress enzymes are widely used as sensitive biomarkers in ecotoxicological assessments to understand the potential threats of environmental pollutants. Studies on PS-NP toxicity have shown that they induce oxidative stress, damage cell membranes, and trigger biochemical changes, leading to inflammation and apoptosis (Wu et al., 2022). In our study, nanoplastics significantly reduced the gene expression levels of Nrf2, NQO1, CAT, SOD, GPX-1, and HO-1 in chicken liver and jejunum, inducing oxidative stress in cells and thereby promoting hepatotoxicity and intestinal toxicity. PS-MPs also caused oxidative stress in chicken thymus (Li et al., 2023), lungs (Lu et al., 2023), and testes (Hou et al., 2022), resulting in tissue damage. These findings indicate that both micro- and nanoplastics exert significant oxidative stress effects on chicken organs, leading to inflammation and injury.

To assess the potential for recovery in chickens following cessation of nanoplastic exposure, we examined the status of key molecular and cellular pathways 21 days post-exposure. Our findings indicate that the TLR4/NF-κB signaling pathway remained persistently activated. Hepatic apoptosis processes continued to be active, whereas apoptosis in the jejunum demonstrated marked amelioration. In terms of autophagic activity, both medium- and high-dose exposure groups exhibited sustained inhibition of autophagy and suppression of the Nrf2 signaling pathway in the liver and jejunum. These results substantiated that the effects of nanoplastics on chickens were not resolved immediately upon cessation of exposure. Further experiments are needed to determine how long these effects persist.

Normal intestinal mucosal immunity relies on the three major intestinal barriers, including the intestinal mucosal epithelial barrier, the immune barrier formed by intestinal immune cells and their secretions, and the biological barrier constructed by the normal intestinal microbiota (Man et al., 2014). Tight junctions are the primary structures constituting the epithelial barrier, providing selective barrier functions against various gastrointestinal pathogens. They mainly consist of Claudin, Occludin, and Zona Occludens (ZO) proteins (Dokladny et al., 2016). Intestinal Mucin2 is a crucial component of the intestinal mucus layer, belonging to the transmembrane mucin family, a and is crucial for preserving the function of the intestinal barrier, regulating microbial balance, and immune modulation (Borisova et al., 2020). Research has indicated that micro/nanoplastic exposure significantly reduces the expression of intestinal mucosal proteins, including tight junction proteins (such as occludin and ZO-1) and mucus-related proteins, thereby impairing intestinal barrier function (Zhang et al., 2023; Kim et al., 2024). Our research indicated that nanoplastics could significantly downregulate the gene expression levels of Mucin2, Claudin1, Occludin (MP21G Group), and ZO-1. After a 21-day recovery period, the impact of nanoplastics on tight junction-related genes showed no significant difference.

The small intestine is the primary site for nutrient digestion and absorption in animals. The height of the intestinal villi and the depth of crypts directly influence the digestive and absorptive functions of the intestinal tract in broilers. An increase in villus height (VH) enhances the digestive and absorptive capacity of the small intestine, while a decrease in crypt depth (CD) indicates a more mature development of the digestive tract. Thus, an increased villus height-to-crypt depth ratio signifies improved intestinal digestion and absorption efficiency (Slifer and Blikslager, 2020). Moreover, intestinal villi serve as the first barrier against harmful antigens and pathogenic bacteria (Duan et al., 2022). Our study revealed that after 21 days of nanoplastic ingestion, both the low-dose and high-dose groups showed a significant increase in VH in the jejunum. Additionally, the low-dose group demonstrated a significant elevation in CD in the same intestinal segment. This paradoxical finding may be attributed to villus hyperplasia caused by intestinal inflammation induced by nanoplastics, which acts as a repair mechanism to restore damaged intestinal mucosa (Feakins et al., 2024). The crypt region houses intestinal stem cells, and an increase in crypt depth may reflect accelerated cell turnover in response to intestinal injury or inflammation. After a 21-day withdrawal period from nanoplastic exposure, VH and VH/CD ratio in the jejunum significantly decreased, while CD in the jejunum significantly increased. This finding may be due to persistent immunosuppressive signals during the recovery phase, potentially hindering the regeneration of villi and crypts (Hou et al., 2025). In summary, nanoplastics negatively affect the intestinal mechanical barrier in chickens, increasing intestinal permeability and raising the risk of pathogenic microbial infections.

Poultry gut microbiota serves as a crucial bridge connecting nutrients and host metabolism, enhancing broiler production performance by influencing intestinal development, nutrient utilization, and immune function (Thomas et al., 2019). Besides, gut microbiota prevents pathogenic colonization through competitive exclusion, production of antibacterial or bactericidal substances, and induction of host immune responses to suppress pathogen infections (Thaiss et al., 2016; Pickard et al., 2017). It also promotes host antiviral defense via the cGAS-STING-IFN-I system (Erttmann et al., 2022).

Microbial community composition analysis revealed that nanoplastics significantly increased the abundance of the dominant bacterial phylum Proteobacteria, Actinobacteriota. Proteobacteria and Actinobacteriota include many opportunistic pathogens such as Escherichia coli, Salmonella, Vibrio cholerae, Helicobacter pylori, and Corynebacterium species, which can easily cause enteritis in animals and subsequently impair intestinal mucosal barrier function (Fu et al., 2023).

At the genus level, NPs significantly increased the abundance of Anaeroplasma, Christensenellaceae R-7 group, Escherichia-Shigella, and Collinsella in the cecum. Anaeroplasma is an opportunistic pathogen that can trigger various host immune responses in human diseases (Zeng et al., 2018). The Christensenellaceae R-7 group, belonging to the phylum Firmicutes, plays a crucial role in gut health through butyrate metabolic pathways. Studies have shown that its butyrate metabolic network is associated with enhanced intestinal barrier function and anti-inflammatory effects (Waters and Ley, 2019; Guo et al., 2020). Nanoplastics were found to promote the growth of Escherichia-Shigella, a Gram-negative bacterium belonging to the Proteobacteria phylum, facilitating the entry of toxic mediators such as lipopolysaccharides into the systemic circulation, triggering inflammatory responses, and exacerbating heart failure (Wang et al., 2021). The increased abundance of Escherichia-Shigella can also elevate intestinal inflammasome NLRP3, activating mucosal immunity, intensifying gut inflammation, and impairing barrier function (Zhao et al., 2022). The expansion of this bacterial group may suppress the growth of butyrate-producing bacteria (e.g., Roseburia, Ruminococcus), reducing short-chain fatty acid (SCFA) production and weakening their restorative effects on the intestinal barrier (e.g., promoting mucus secretion and tight junction protein expression) (Niu et al., 2023). Similarly, in mice exposed to microplastics, the abundance of Escherichia-Shigella in the gut microbiota significantly increased (Jiang et al., 2021). Collinsella is considered a pathogenic bacterium, positively correlated with intestinal permeability marker PV-1, suggesting it may promote gut barrier dysfunction, allowing intestinal contents to enter the systemic circulation and trigger inflammation (Gargari et al., 2024). It also shows positive correlations with liver function markers (e.g., ALP, ALT, and bilirubin), indicating potential effects on liver dysfunction through increased intestinal permeability or direct hepatic action (Gargari et al., 2024; Purohit et al., 2024). Overall, these results suggest that nanoplastics lead to a rise in the abundance of harmful bacteria while reducing beneficial ones, leading to chicken cecal dysbiosis.

After discontinuing NPs for 21 days, analysis of the microbial community composition showed that at the genus level, NPs caused a significant increase in the abundance of Flavobacteriaceae_uncultured, [Eubacterium]_coprostanoligenes_group_norank, and Phascolarctobacterium in the chicken cecum, while the abundance of Barnesiella and Shuttleworthia significantly decreased. Many members of the Flavobacteriaceae_uncultured family are pathogenic. When the gut microbiota is disrupted due to antibiotic misuse or water pollution (e.g., glyphosate exposure), some Flavobacteriaceae bacteria may overproliferate, becoming opportunistic pathogens that trigger intestinal inflammation or infection (Suppa et al., 2020). [Eubacterium]_coprostanoligenes_group_norank is a probiotic that produces butyrate, which plays a key role in energy homeostasis, immune regulation, and suppression of intestinal inflammation (Mukherjee et al., 2020). Eubacterium_coprostanoligenes_group can reduce cholesterol levels and improve liver and blood lipid abnormalities by preventing diet-induced obesity and metabolic disorders (obesity, hypertension, diabetes) (Wei et al., 2021). Phascolarctobacterium belongs to the Firmicutes phylum and can ferment to produce SCFAs in the gut. SCFAs can protect the intestinal mucosa, provide energy for epithelial cells, reduce systemic inflammation, and enhance digestive activity (von Rosenvinge et al., 2013). Phascolarctobacterium effectively inhibits the growth and colonization of pathogenic bacteria by competing for nutrients (Nagao-Kitamoto et al., 2020). Barnesiella, a member of Bacteroidetes, stimulates T-cell proliferation, promotes the development of innate and adaptive intestinal immunity, and may inhibit the colonization of vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus faecium, potentially aiding in the clearance of drug-resistant bacteria (Ubeda et al., 2013). Besides, it may have protective effects against diarrhea and stress (Fan et al., 2021; Zheng et al., 2021). Shuttleworthia, a beneficial Firmicutes bacterium, is highly positively correlated with microbial protein, propionate, total VFA, glucosidase, acetate, and butyrate (Hao et al., 2021). These results suggest that after 21 days of discontinuation, the microbiota at the phylum level tended to stabilize with no significant differences among groups. However, at the genus level, harmful bacteria remained elevated while beneficial bacteria were reduced, indicating that nanoplastics continued to affect the chickens.

The functional analysis of the microbiota based on PICRUSt2 software indicated that the main enrichments were in the Nervous System, Genetic Information Processing, and Infectious Diseases. KEGG database analysis revealed that the Infectious Diseases pathway typically included gene functions associated with pathogen infection and host-pathogen interactions. Nanoplastics significantly increased the abundance of Escherichia-Shigella in the cecum. The impact of Escherichia spp. is not limited to the gut but can also affect brain function through the microbiota-gut-brain axis (Grenham et al., 2011). Studies have found that abnormal increases in Escherichia spp. are associated with the occurrence of mental disorders such as anxiety and depression (Braz et al., 2020; Mansour et al., 2023). The significant reduction in the abundance of Nervous System functional genes may be linked to Escherichia-Shigella. After 21 days of discontinuing nanoplastics, the abundance of functional genes related to Cellular Processes and Signaling, Enzyme Families, Excretory System, Nervous System, Endocrine System, and Transport and Catabolism significantly decreased due to the nanoplastics. This further indicates that nanoplastics have negative effects on chickens.

This study systematically revealed the multidimensional toxic effects and underlying mechanisms of NPs on chickens. Although NPs did not significantly affect growth performance, they caused notable damage to organs such as the liver and intestines by inducing oxidative stress, inflammatory responses, and metabolic disorders. Some of these toxic effects persisted even after discontinuation of exposure.

Besides, we found that NP exposure significantly suppressed serum antioxidant enzyme activities and elevated MDA levels, indicating aggravated lipid peroxidation. Liver transcriptome analysis revealed that abnormal activation of the glycerophospholipid metabolism pathway could contribute to toxic effects through a dual mechanism: on one hand, phospholipase A2-mediated arachidonic acid metabolism promotes the production of pro-inflammatory mediators (e.g., prostaglandins), forming a positive feedback loop with TLR4/NF-κB pathway activation (Hidalgo et al., 2024); on the other hand, oxidation products of polyunsaturated fatty acids could exacerbate mitochondrial membrane lipid peroxidation, leading to aberrant expression of respiratory chain complex I subunits (NDUFB6/NDUFB5/NDUFS8) and consequent electron transport chain dysfunction with ROS burst (Bridges et al., 2023). This vicious cycle of metabolic-oxidative stress was particularly evident in the downregulation of cholesterol synthesis key enzymes (MSMO1/DHCR7), which not only compromised membrane stability but may also enhanced ferroptosis susceptibility by altering 7-dehydrocholesterol levels (Sun et al., 2023), consistent with upregulated hepatic apoptosis markers Bax/Caspase-3.

NPs persistently activate the TLR4/MyD88/NF-κB pathway, leading to excessive secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-1β and TNF-α, which are closely associated with the proliferation of opportunistic pathogens like Escherichia-Shigella in the gut microbiota (Li et al., 2024b). Notably, the Nrf2 pathway is subjected to dual inhibition: on one hand, sustained NF-κB activation competitively depletes Nrf2 co-activators; on the other hand, NPs directly interfere with Nrf2 nuclear translocation, reducing the expression of phase II detoxifying enzymes such as HO-1 and NQO1 (Hu and Palić, 2020). This crosstalk between oxidative and inflammatory pathways may explain the persistence of liver injury after drug withdrawal.

Our study also revealed that NPs could disrupt the intestinal mechanical barrier by downregulating tight junction proteins (Claudin1/Occludin), which aligned with abnormalities in the “Environmental Adaptation” pathway predicted by microbial metabolic function. The characteristic intestinal microbiota dysbiosis was manifested by an increased abundance of Proteobacteria, whose LPS secretion exacerbates Kupffer cell activation in the liver via the portal circulation (Zhang et al., 2022c; Szentirmai et al., 2025). Notably, the recovery phase showed an increase in cecal SCFA-producing bacteria (Phascolarctobacterium), which could restore intestinal barrier integrity to some extent via the PPARγ pathway (Louis and Flint, 2009; Ma et al., 2022; Noori et al., 2024). However, persistent abnormalities in the “Genetic Information Processing” pathway suggest that epigenetic modifications may contribute to long-term toxicity.

Post-exposure, cardiac index recovery and improvement in some intestinal indicators were observed, suggesting a threshold effect of NPs' physical damage. However, persistent suppression of the hepatic Nrf2 pathway, activation of the TLR4 pathway, and abnormalities in microbial functional genes may be related to the bioaccumulation of NPs in tissues (Wang et al., 2024). This delayed toxic effect was particularly prominent in the sustained low expression of the drug-metabolizing enzyme CYP51A1, potentially affecting steroid hormone synthesis and xenobiotic clearance (Guengerich, 2008).

Overall, this study provides hitherto undocumented evidence of the molecular mechanisms by which NPs induce multi-organ damage in chickens through the inflammation, oxidative stress, apoptosis, and autophagy inhibition in the liver and jejunum; disrupting mitochondrial respiration in the liver and causing intestinal barrier dysfunction accompanied by dysbiosis, providing a theoretical basis for assessing the threat of plastic pollution to livestock health. Future research should further explore: 1) the interaction mode between NPs and mitochondrial complex I; 2) the specific regulatory network linking abnormal cholesterol metabolism and ferroptosis; 3) the intervention potential of gut microbiota functional remodeling in toxicity mitigation.

Despite the significant findings presented in this study, we acknowledge several limitations that warrant consideration. Our investigation utilized young chicks as experimental animals. It is crucial to recognize that different growth stages in poultry can exhibit varying sensitivities to toxic substances. Our findings, therefore, may not be directly generalizable to all growth phases of poultry, and future studies should aim to explore these potential stage-specific differences in NP toxicity. Moreover, this study did not extensively investigate the toxicokinetic profile of NPs within the chickens. A detailed understanding of these toxicokinetic aspects would provide a more comprehensive mechanistic explanation for the observed effects and could help to predict potential long-term health impacts or bioaccumulation.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: In summary, this study demonstrates that nanoplastics negatively affect various aspects of chickens, including serum parameters, liver function, intestinal barrier, and gut microbiota. Some of these effects persist even post-exposure. Further research is needed to elucidate the specific mechanisms and long-term impacts, providing a scientific basis for developing effective prevention and control measures.

sec.title: Ethics approval
sec.p: All experimental procedures adhered to the standards set forth in the National Research Council's "Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals" and received approval from the Laboratory Animal Welfare and Ethical Reviewing Committee at Xinyang Agriculture and Forestry University (Approval ID: 202403011).

sec.title: Data and model availability statement
sec.p: The data availability statement should state that data will be made available upon request.
